stories.title: Roland Garros Day 1 — The Matches, the Night Session, and What to Watch Sunday

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Sunday night on Chatrier: Djokovic vs. Mpetshi Perricard.

Not before 8:15 PM Paris. The rematch of Djokovic's only clay match of 2026 — which he lost at Rome.

The biggest opening night in years. 🎾 #RolandGarros #Djokovic

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Roland Garros Day 1 is Sunday. Here's the card:

Sinner vs. Tabur — Chatrier, 11 AM Paris
Gauff (defending) vs. Townsend — Day 1
Djokovic vs. Perricard — Chatrier night, not before 8:15 PM

It begins. 🎾 #RolandGarros

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:00 AM ET

Tomorrow, Roland Garros 2026 begins.

The main draw opens at 11:00 AM local time in Paris on Sunday, May 24, and the order of play is already turning heads. Jannik Sinner opens his quest for the career Grand Slam against French wild card Clément Tabur on Court Philippe-Chatrier — the main stage, on the biggest occasion of his tennis career. Coco Gauff, the defending champion, faces fellow American Taylor Townsend in what should be a crowd-pleasing first-round clash.

But the match everyone is talking about happens at night.

Not before 8:15 PM Paris time, Novak Djokovic walks out onto Chatrier to face Giovanni Mpetshi Perricard under the lights. It's a rematch: Perricard was the player who beat Djokovic at Rome in May — the only clay-court match Djokovic played all season. Perricard has said he's "ambitious" about the match. Djokovic says his preparation has been positive.

Iga Swiatek, Elena Rybakina, and Elina Svitolina all make their main draw debuts on Monday. This is a three-day first round, running May 24 through 26.

Women's final is June 7. Men's final is June 8. The tournament that has been months in the making starts tomorrow at 11:00 AM Paris time.

Who are you most looking forward to watching on Day 1?

---

article.headline: Day 1 Arrives: Sinner, Gauff, and a Djokovic Night Session to Kick Off Roland Garros 2026

stories.title: Sabalenka — "Feeling 100%": The Physical Recovery and Mental Reset

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
Sabalenka on her 2025 Roland Garros final loss:

"My emotions were destroying my game."

She says she's fixed that. She says she's feeling 100% physically.

The No. 1 seed is ready for another shot at the title she's never won. 🎾 #RolandGarros #Sabalenka

facebook.long_form:
Window: 12:30 PM ET

Aryna Sabalenka came to Paris knowing this tournament is the one she hasn't won. This year, she says she's ready to fix that — and she's been unusually specific about why.

She struggled physically during the clay-court swing. A hip and lower back issue troubled her through Rome, where she lost in the third round to Sorana Cirstea. But in media day interviews at Roland Garros this week, she made a point of separating that from where she stands now. "I struggled at the beginning of the clay-court season physically, to be honest, but right now I feel 100 per cent," she said.

More striking was what she said about the 2025 Roland Garros final — a match she led and lost to Coco Gauff 6-7(5), 6-2, 6-4. Sabalenka has thought a lot about what went wrong inside that match. The conclusion she reached: "My emotions were destroying my game, and my level was dropping dramatically when I would just start overreacting on everything."

Her strategy now involves keeping what's happening internally hidden from opponents. "Making sure that my opponent doesn't see what's going on in my head — and at the same time, to perform better and to stay in the zone, it was a huge improvement over the years in my career and really helped me to level up."

This is the version of Sabalenka that arrives as No. 1 seed and opens against Jessica Bouzas Maneiro. If she runs the gauntlet of her projected draw, she could face Gauff again in the semifinals.

Will Sabalenka win her first Roland Garros in 2026? She certainly seems to believe she's ready.

---

stories.title: Players Stage Media Protest Over Roland Garros Prize Money

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Roland Garros generated 395M euros in 2025.

Players got 14.3% of it. Standard events pay 22%.

Sabalenka and Gauff are limiting press conferences to 15 minutes in protest. The tournament says prize money stays the same.

The match hasn't started. 🎾 #RolandGarros

facebook.long_form:
Window: 3:00 PM ET

Roland Garros hasn't started, and it already has its biggest story — and it's happening off the court.

Top players have spent the past week protesting what they describe as an unfair share of the tournament's revenues. Roland Garros generated 395 million euros in 2025 — a 14 percent increase from the year before. Player prize money represents 14.3 percent of that total. At a standard ATP or WTA event, players receive 22 percent of event revenue.

Players, led by world No. 1 Aryna Sabalenka and defending champion Coco Gauff, are demanding a 22 percent increase in prize money. The response from the tournament: no. Tournament director Amélie Mauresmo confirmed this week that prize money for 2026 will not change, despite a 9.5 percent increase already baked in from last year's levels.

The players have responded with a media protest: press conferences this week have been limited to 15 minutes per session, rather than the standard format. Sabalenka put the reasoning plainly: "I am No. 1, I must set an example and fight for all the players."

This is not a boycott of the tournament itself. All top players are competing. But the tension between the players and the organization running one of the sport's most commercially successful events is the conversation happening in the corridors at Roland Garros this week — alongside everything else.

Do you think the players' prize money demands at Roland Garros are fair?

---

stories.title: Yannick Noah Day — Roland Garros's Charity Celebration Closes Opening Week

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Today at Roland Garros: Yannick Noah Day.

Team Noah vs. Team Mauresmo on Chatrier from 2 PM. Live music. Children's sessions. Annual charity day.

Tomorrow, the main draw starts.

One last celebration before the real thing. 🎾 #RolandGarros

facebook.long_form:
Window: 6:00 PM ET

Roland Garros has one more celebration before the tournament begins in earnest tomorrow — and it is happening today.

Yannick Noah Day is the annual charity day at Roland Garros, and it is one of the most accessible days on the tennis calendar for fans. Gates are open from 11:00 AM local time, and fans can attend exhibitions across all courts, meet players in "Play with" sessions on Courts Suzanne-Lenglen and Simonne-Mathieu, and enjoy live performances from artists Lenie, Jungeli, and Emma.

The main event: "The Battle," an exhibition match on Court Philippe-Chatrier from 2:00 to 3:30 PM Paris time, pitting Team Yannick Noah against Team Amélie Mauresmo. Two legends of French tennis. One afternoon.

Today caps off Opening Week — a stretch that has included four days of qualifying, the draw ceremony on Thursday, and Wednesday's unforgettable "Gaël & Friends" farewell for Gaël Monfils on Chatrier. Tomorrow, all of that gives way to Day 1 of the main draw.

The party before the tennis. Who's tuning in?

---

stories.title: Swiatek's Ostapenko Problem — The 6-0 Nemesis Lurking in Round Three

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Iga Swiatek: 4x Roland Garros champion.

Jelena Ostapenko vs. Swiatek all-time: 6-0.

Ostapenko is in Swiatek's projected third round.

One match. One nemesis. The draw has handed Swiatek her most dangerous early hurdle. 🎾 #RolandGarros #Swiatek

facebook.long_form:
Window: 9:00 PM ET

Of all the dangerous names in the Roland Garros women's draw, the one that should worry Iga Swiatek the most might be the one sitting in her projected third round: Jelena Ostapenko.

Swiatek is the No. 3 seed. She is a four-time Roland Garros champion. She is one of the most decorated players of her generation on clay. She opens against Emerson Jones on Monday.

And then, if everything goes according to seeding and form, she meets Jelena Ostapenko.

The head-to-head record between them is 6-0 in Ostapenko's favour, all-time. Six matches. Six wins for Ostapenko. Only one of those victories came on clay — but the record is the record. Swiatek has not found an answer to Ostapenko's game across any surface in any of their meetings.

Ostapenko is the 2017 Roland Garros champion. She won here with the kind of high-risk, all-court aggression that disrupts the tactical rhythms of more consistent players — precisely what makes her so dangerous against Swiatek in particular.

This isn't a guaranteed outcome. Ostapenko has to win two matches first. The draw could open differently. But if Ostapenko is in the third round and Swiatek is the No. 3 seed, this is the match the Roland Garros women's bracket should be watching.

Does Swiatek finally crack the Ostapenko code at Roland Garros?

---

article.headline: Swiatek's Ostapenko Problem: Six Wins, Zero Losses, and a Potential Round Three at Roland Garros
